quotes.text: In that day. In the day of the Lord, meaning the time of the Lord's second coming (see 2:12).
quotes.speaker: Donald W. Parry
quotes.attr: Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.241

quotes.text: I will guard it night and day. A destroyer continually waits to attack, so Jehovah will watch over his vineyard constantly. Similarly, "He that keepeth Israel shall neither slumber nor sleep. The Lord is thy keeper: the Lord is thy shade upon thy right hand. The sun shall not smite thee by day, nor the moon by night. The Lord shall preserve thee from all evil: he shall preserve thy soul" (Ps. 121:4-7; Isa. 25:4-5).
quotes.speaker: Donald W. Parry
quotes.attr: Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.242

quotes.text: I the Lord do keep it. The Lord himself is the caretaker and the guardian of the vineyard. This is a contrast to Isa. 5:5, in which he no longer protects Israel because of her nonproductivity, or wickedness.
quotes.speaker: Donald W. Parry
quotes.attr: Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.242

quotes.text: I will water it every moment. For the meaning of water here, see Isa. 8:6. The expression every moment indicates that the Lord will give divine attention to the vineyard.
quotes.speaker: Donald W. Parry
quotes.attr: Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.242

quotes.text: I would burn them. The Lord at his coming will act against the wicked, overcoming all who oppose him by destroying them with fire (10:17; 26:11); this destruction will be as simple as burning the briers and thorns in a vineyard.
quotes.speaker: Donald W. Parry
quotes.attr: Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.242

quotes.text: Fury is not in me. The wrath of the Lord, which once hung over the unrighteous of his covenant people, has been lifted, and now he seeks to defend his vineyard (contrast Isa. 5:5-7, 25).
quotes.speaker: Donald W. Parry
quotes.attr: Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.242

notes: Ultimately, each person who has entered into the covenant of peace in the temple, which is a covenant with Christ, to be modeled like him, becomes the Tree of Life. Therefore in the temple, you are allowed to take root and grow and bring forth fruit, and these are the ones who fill the earth, therefore Eden is not just filled, or paradisiacal glory, it's not just filled with fruitful and productive trees, but it is filled with Israel, who is now fruitful and productive, having posterity as numerous as sand upon the seashore, or the stars in heaven, if you could count them, and they are now like the trees of Eden also. The only ones who can rise and live in the millennium and endure on into is the celestial kingdom, are those who have made the covenant of peace in the place where you make the peace, then they are the ones who take root and begin to grow and to fill the earth as Eden. Symbolically speaking, Alma was right on target in Alma Chapter 32, when he had you plant a seed and had that seed grow up and when it was finished, remember it was the tree of life.

quotes.text: Jacob will take root. The vineyard (Jacob) will take root, or will become firmly established, in the promised land; after the vineyard matures (blossoms and buds), it will fill "the world with fruit," which may speak of literal offspring (2 Ne. 3:4; Gen. 30:2) or possibly righteous deeds (Jer. 17:7-8).
quotes.speaker: Donald W. Parry
quotes.attr: Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.242

quotes.text: Hath he smitten him. The verse is difficult without the grammatical subjects and objects, so we supply them here in brackets: "Hath he [God] smitten him [Israel], as he [God] smote those [Israel's oppressors] that smote him [Israel]? or is he [Israel] slain according to the slaughter of them [Israel's oppressors] that are slain by him [God]?" The two rhetorical questions show that even in Israel's gross wickedness, the Lord did not completely destroy Israel as he did her oppressors (Assyria and Babylon). Rather, the Lord has scattered Israel as a chastisement to bring about a repentant people.
quotes.speaker: Donald W. Parry
quotes.attr: Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.242

quotes.text: The east wind (v. 8) is probably the hot sirocco that blows out of the Arabian desert and destroys vegetation. Usually arriving in the early summer months while the plants are still young and tender, the sirocco destroys crops and brings chaos to the seas as it carries sand and debris before it. (See Isa. 14:7; Hosea 13:15; Ezek. 27:26; Jer. 18:17.) It is clear that Isaiah is promising severe treatment for Israel.
quotes.speaker: Victor L. Ludlow
quotes.attr: Victor L. Ludlow, Isaiah: Prophet, Seer, and Poet, p.256

quotes.text: By warfare and exile you contend with him. The Lord punishes Israel by sending armies to war against her and by sending her into exile.
quotes.speaker: Donald W. Parry
quotes.attr: Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.242

quotes.text: By this . . . shall the iniquity of Jacob be purged. "Warfare" and "exile"- God's chastisements upon Israel-will purge Israel from her iniquity: "I, the Lord, will contend with Zion . . . and chasten her until she overcomes and is clean before me" (D&C 90:36).
quotes.speaker: Donald W. Parry
quotes.attr: Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.243

notes: Where do you start to pull down Israel? At the temple, The Savior came in, last week of his life. He looked at the Jews, and he said "your house will be left unto you, desolate." D&C 112:25 start from His house
them on fire: for it [is] a people of no understanding: therefore he that made them will not have mercy on them, and he that formed them will shew them no favour.

There are 6 Woes " 1st Woe 28:1 - to the crown of the pride to the drunkards of Ephraim. " 2nd Woe 29:1 - Woe to Ariel. " 3rd Woe 29:15 - unto them that seek deep to hide their counsels from the Lord. " 4th Woe 30:1 - to the rebellious children saith the Lord that take counsel, but not have me. " 5th Woe 31:1 - to them that go down to Egypt for help. " 6th Woe 33:1 - to the that spoilest and thou widst not spoil.

Notice at the end of Apocalypse, at the last song, watch where everybody ends up - the Temple. So, when the Apocalypse concludes, Israel, who have been in Egypt, they've been in Assyria, and again, I feel Egypt and Assyria there are used to representing the ends of the earth and every place the earth is scattered; where do they end up? At the temple.

they shall come which were ready to perish in the land of Assyria, and the outcasts in the land of Egypt. The scattered children of Israel, at the point of perishing spiritually, will hearken to the trumpet and
Isaiah Prophesies of the Destruction of Ephraim (28:1-8)

quotes.text: The Northern Kingdom of Israel, under the leadership of the tribe of Ephraim, will fall under condemnation because of pride and wickedness (28:1). As a result, a mighty and strong nation (Assyria; 28:2) has been commissioned to accomplish eagerly and swiftly the judgments of the Lord (28:3-4). In the last days, the "residue" of the children of Israel will consider the Lord to be "a crown of glory" and "a diadem of beauty" (28:5); they will find wisdom and strength in their association with him (28:6).
quotes.speaker: Donald W. Parry
quotes.attr: Donald W. Parry, Jay A. Parry, and Tina M. Peterson, Understanding Isaiah, p.246
